name: 5-Note Scale (1-2-3-4-5-4-3-2-1)
description: The classic warm-up. Sing up 5 notes of the major scale and back down. Focus on even tone and smooth transitions between notes.
goodFor: Warming up with a simple 1-2-3-4-5-4-3-2-1 stepwise pattern that sits comfortably in one position. The short range means you can focus purely on even tone and smooth connections between neighboring notes.

name: Major Triad (1-3-5-3-1)
description: Sing each note of the major triad ascending and descending. Focus on clean pitch and even tone across the intervals.
goodFor: Locking your pitch onto the 1st, 3rd, and 5th scale degrees — the three notes that define a major chord. The skips between notes train your ear to hear chord tones and land each interval cleanly.

name: Octave Arpeggio (1-3-5-8-5-3-1)
description: Full octave arpeggio. Great for building range and bridging registers. Keep your support steady through the top note.
goodFor: Singing 1-3-5-8-5-3-1 takes you through a full octave on chord tones, so you're likely crossing your vocal break somewhere in the middle. This exercise trains you to keep one consistent sound through that transition.

name: Chromatic Scale (5 Half Steps)
description: Chromatic half-step exercise. Excellent for pitch accuracy and ear training. Keep each note clean and distinct.
goodFor: Moving in half steps (semitones) through five notes. Because each pitch is so close together, any intonation drift is immediately obvious — making this a precision exercise for your pitch accuracy.

name: Fifth Interval Jump (1-5-1-5-1)
description: Practice jumping a perfect fifth. Focus on nailing the interval without scooping. Builds interval recognition.
goodFor: Repeatedly jumping root-to-fifth (7 semitones) and back. This specific interval appears constantly in melodies, so nailing it here means fewer pitch wobbles when you encounter it in songs.

name: Major Scale (Full Octave)
description: Full major scale up and down one octave. The foundation of Western music. Focus on even spacing and consistent tone.
goodFor: Running all eight notes of the major scale up and down. Unlike the 5-note version, this covers a full octave, so you practice maintaining consistent tone and intonation across a wider span.

name: Minor Triad (1-b3-5-b3-1)
description: Minor triad arpeggio. Practice the darker tonality with clean pitch. Notice the difference from the major triad.
goodFor: The 1-b3-5 triad has a smaller gap between root and third (3 semitones vs 4 in major). Training this darker interval sharpens your ear for the subtle difference and helps you sing minor passages in tune.

name: Dominant 7th Arpeggio (1-3-5-b7-5-3-1)
description: Dominant seventh chord arpeggio. Adds the flat-7th for a bluesy, jazzy quality. Great for genre versatility.
goodFor: Adding the b7 to the major triad (1-3-5-b7-5-3-1) introduces an interval you won't find in basic scale exercises. This chord shape shows up in blues, jazz, and pop turnarounds.

name: Staccato Repeated Note
description: Repeat the same note with short, bouncy staccato. Builds diaphragm control and clean onset. Think 'ha ha ha ha ha'.
goodFor: Hitting the same note five times with short, detached attacks. Each onset requires a quick diaphragm engagement — there's no momentum from a melody to carry you. Isolates your breath-onset coordination.

name: Sustained Single Tone
description: Hold a single note as long and steady as possible. Focus on consistent airflow, no wobble. Build your breath support foundation.
goodFor: Holding one single note for as long as possible. With no melody to distract you, every flaw in your airflow, pitch stability, and tone consistency is exposed. The simplest and most honest vocal test.

name: Ascending Thirds
description: Weaving thirds pattern. Builds agility and interval awareness. Keep it smooth and connected.
goodFor: Weaving up and down in thirds (e.g., C-E-D-F-E-G) instead of stepwise motion. This zigzag pattern mimics real melodic writing and forces your voice to handle quick direction changes at every note.

name: Octave Jump (1-8-1)
description: Jump a full octave and back. The ultimate interval challenge. Keep both notes in tune without scooping.
goodFor: Jumping a full 12 semitones from root to octave and back — the widest common interval. There's nowhere to hide: you either nail both notes cleanly or you don't. Builds courage for dramatic leaps in songs.

name: Descending 5-Note Scale
description: Start from the 5th and descend. Great for practicing head-to-chest transitions. Keep the tone consistent as you come down.
goodFor: Starting on the 5th and stepping down to the root. Most singers lose support and tone quality when descending. This specifically trains you to stay engaged and avoid going flat on the way down.

name: Lip Trill / Bubble Scale
description: Do this exercise on a lip trill (bubble lips). Relaxes tension, warms up the voice gently. Perfect first exercise of any session.
goodFor: Running the 5-note scale while buzzing your lips. The trill creates back-pressure that naturally balances your airflow and cord closure — you can't force or strain through a lip trill, so it self-corrects bad habits.

name: Pentatonic Scale
description: The 5-note pentatonic scale used in pop, R&B, and rock. No awkward half steps. Great for riff building.
goodFor: The 1-2-3-5-6 scale (no 4th or 7th) eliminates the tricky half-step intervals. This is the exact scale behind most pop hooks, R&B riffs, and rock solos — learning it as a pattern builds your riffing vocabulary.

name: Natural Minor Scale (Full Octave)
description: Full natural minor scale. Practice the darker, more emotional tonality. Essential for classical and contemporary styles.
goodFor: All eight notes of the natural minor scale across a full octave. The b3, b6, and b7 give it a different feel from major — practicing this scale trains your ear to handle both tonalities equally well.

name: Messa di Voce (Swell)
description: Hold a single note. Start quiet, crescendo to full volume, then diminuendo back to quiet. The ultimate dynamic control exercise.
goodFor: Going from pianissimo to fortissimo and back on a single held note. This demands independent control of airflow and cord tension — you're adjusting volume without changing pitch, tone, or vowel shape.

name: Rapid 5-Note Scale
description: The classic 5-note scale at double speed. Builds vocal agility and coordination. Don't sacrifice pitch for speed.
goodFor: The same 1-2-3-4-5-4-3-2-1 pattern as the standard warm-up, but at double tempo. Speed reveals any tension or coordination issues that slower singing masks. Trains your voice to stay relaxed at pace.

name: Siren Slide (1 to 8)
description: Glide smoothly from root to octave like a siren. Focus on seamless register transitions. Don't break the sound.
goodFor: A continuous chromatic glide from root to octave — no discrete pitches, just a smooth slide. This exposes exactly where your register break is and trains you to smooth it out instead of flipping.

name: Fourth Interval Jump (1-4-1-4-1)
description: Practice the perfect fourth interval. A natural-sounding jump found everywhere in music. Keep both notes centered.
goodFor: Bouncing between root and fourth (5 semitones) repeatedly. The perfect fourth is one of the most natural intervals in music — think of 'Here Comes the Bride.' Ingraining it here makes it automatic in songs.

name: Two-Octave Arpeggio
description: Extended two-octave arpeggio for advanced range work. Challenges your full register from chest through head voice.
goodFor: Arpeggios spanning two full octaves (1-3-5-8-10-12-15 and back). You'll move through chest, mix, and head voice in a single pass — this is for singers who can already handle one-octave exercises and want to push further.

name: Diminished 7th Arpeggio
description: Each interval is a minor third. The symmetrical pattern trains your ear for unusual intervals common in classical and jazz.
goodFor: Every interval in this arpeggio is a minor third (3 semitones), creating a symmetrical, unsettling pattern. Because nothing sounds 'settled,' your ear can't rely on tonal gravity — it's pure pitch memory training.

name: Tongue Trill Scale
description: Rolled R (tongue trill) through the 5-note scale. An alternative to lip trills that works the same relaxation benefits with different coordination.
goodFor: A rolled R through the 5-note scale. Unlike lip trills, the tongue trill adds coordination for the tongue itself while still creating the semi-occluded back-pressure that promotes balanced phonation.

name: Humming Scale
description: Hum with closed lips through the 5-note scale. Feel the vibrations in your face (mask resonance). The gentlest possible warm-up.
goodFor: Closed-mouth 5-note scale where all sound resonates through your nasal cavity and face. You'll feel exactly where your voice vibrates — forehead, cheekbones, nose — building awareness of forward placement.

name: Nay Twang Exercise
description: Sing 'Nay' with an exaggerated twang. Builds forward placement and bright resonance. Essential for contemporary singing and belt technique.
goodFor: The 'Nay' syllable on a 5-note scale, exaggerating the nasal twang. This specific sound narrows the vocal tract above the cords, creating a bright, cutting resonance used in belting, pop, and musical theater.

name: Vocal Fry to Chest Voice
description: Start in vocal fry, then gradually add air to transition into full chest voice. Builds cord closure and clean onset.
goodFor: Starting three repeated notes from vocal fry (the creaky, low rumble) and transitioning into clear chest voice. This teaches your vocal folds to close gently before adding air — the healthiest way to begin a note.

name: Yawn-Sigh
description: Start high and sigh downward through your full range. Releases tension and helps find relaxed head voice. Think of a lazy yawn.
goodFor: A chromatic slide from the top of your range all the way down, mimicking a yawn. The yawn position naturally lowers the larynx and opens the throat, releasing accumulated tension after intense practice.

name: Descending Staccato 5-Note
description: Staccato bouncing from the 5th descending. Builds agility and diaphragmatic engagement with descending patterns.
goodFor: Short, bounced notes on each step from the 5th down to the root. Combines the breath control demands of staccato with the support challenges of descending — two difficulties in one pattern.

name: Wide Arpeggio (1-5-8-5-1)
description: Skips the third and jumps straight to the fifth and octave. Challenging intervals that build range and intonation simultaneously.
goodFor: Jumping root-fifth-octave (1-5-8-5-1) skips the third entirely, creating two large leaps back-to-back. This is harder than a standard arpeggio because there's no middle note to step through.

name: Semitone Trill
description: Rapidly alternate between two adjacent notes. Builds laryngeal flexibility and vocal agility. Start slow and increase speed.
goodFor: Rapidly alternating between two notes a half step apart (like a classical trill). This requires your larynx to make tiny, fast adjustments — building the flexibility needed for ornaments and fast passages.

name: Blues Scale
description: The classic blues scale with the blue note (b5). Essential for gospel, R&B, blues, and rock. Feel the soul in those chromatic passing tones.
goodFor: The 1-b3-4-b5-5-b7 scale includes the 'blue note' (b5) — that tense, gritty note between the 4th and 5th. Practicing this specific scale gives you the raw material for gospel, R&B, and blues improvisation.

name: Major 6th Interval Jump
description: Jump a major sixth — the opening interval of 'My Bonnie Lies Over the Ocean.' A wide, beautiful interval that challenges intonation.
goodFor: Leaping 9 semitones (a major sixth) repeatedly. This is the interval that opens 'My Bonnie Lies Over the Ocean' — wide enough to challenge your intonation but common enough to appear regularly in repertoire.

name: 9-Note Rapid Run
description: A blazing fast run through 9 notes and beyond. The ultimate agility test. Speed comes from relaxation, not force.
goodFor: A rapid run spanning 9+ scale degrees and back, reaching beyond one octave. At tempo, this is a speed-and-accuracy gauntlet — if you can stay relaxed and in tune through this, shorter runs will feel easy.

name: Vowel Modification (Ah-Eh-Ee-Oh-Oo)
description: Hold a single pitch while cycling through all five Italian vowels. Trains vowel purity and consistent resonance across different mouth shapes.
goodFor: Cycling through Ah-Eh-Ee-Oh-Oo on a single held pitch. Each vowel reshapes your mouth and throat differently — this trains you to keep your pitch and resonance consistent regardless of the vowel you're singing.

name: Sob / Cry Exercise
description: Sing with a gentle 'crying' quality. This tilts the larynx forward, thins the vocal folds, and helps access head voice with emotion.
goodFor: Descending 5 notes with a gentle crying or sobbing quality. The 'cry' tilts your thyroid cartilage forward, naturally thinning your vocal folds — this is the specific mechanism that lets you sing higher without straining.

name: Belt Arpeggio (1-3-5-8)
description: Ascend-only arpeggio finishing on the octave. Build toward holding the top note with chest-dominant mix. The money note exercise.
goodFor: An ascend-only arpeggio ending on the octave and holding it. The entire exercise builds toward that top note, training you to arrive with enough breath, support, and cord closure to sustain a powerful belt.

name: Mum Scale (Mix Bridge)
description: Sing 'Mum' through a full octave scale. The nasal M consonant helps bridge chest and head voice smoothly through the passaggio.
goodFor: Singing 'Mum' through a full octave scale. The M consonant channels sound through your nose, and the short 'uh' vowel keeps your throat open. Together, they guide you through the passaggio without cracking.

name: Bratty Nay Octave (1-8-1)
description: Sing 'Nay' with a bratty, nasal quality on an octave jump. The twang keeps mix voice engaged through the break. Embrace the ugly sound.
goodFor: A bratty 'Nay' on an octave jump (1-8-1). The exaggerated nasal twang keeps your cords engaged through the break. The wide interval forces you to maintain that engagement across your full range.

name: Mix Voice Slide (1 to 10)
description: Slide smoothly from chest voice up through the passaggio into head voice. The goal is a seamless, connected sound with no break or flip.
goodFor: A continuous slide spanning over an octave from chest through mix into head. Because it's a slide (not discrete notes), you feel exactly where your voice wants to break — and learn to smooth through it.

name: Gee Scale (Mix Voice)
description: Sing 'Gee' through the 5-note scale. The G consonant creates cord compression that helps maintain chest voice quality as you ascend into mix.
goodFor: Singing 'Gee' on the 5-note scale. The G consonant creates a brief glottal compression that maintains cord closure as you ascend — preventing the breathy flip that happens when cords separate in the passaggio.

name: Hooty Oo Scale
description: Sing a 5-note scale on a hooty 'oo' vowel. The round, owl-like vowel naturally engages head voice resonance without strain.
goodFor: Singing 'Oo' with an owl-like, hooty quality on the 5-note scale. The rounded lip position and back vowel naturally thin the vocal folds and engage head resonance — making it almost impossible to strain.

name: Falsetto Slide Down
description: Start in light falsetto at the top and glide down through your range. Builds control and awareness of your upper register. Keep it light.
goodFor: Starting in light falsetto and gliding chromatically down to your low range. This specifically strengthens the top of your voice and trains a smooth handoff from falsetto back into chest voice.

name: Head Voice Arpeggio (light)
description: Sing the octave arpeggio with a light, heady tone. Focus on keeping the sound forward and resonant without adding chest weight.
goodFor: A full octave arpeggio (1-3-5-8-5-3-1) sung with a light, heady tone. Unlike the standard arpeggio, the focus here is keeping the sound forward and resonant without adding chest weight as you come back down.

name: Ee Head Voice Scale
description: Full octave scale on 'ee' vowel. The narrow vowel naturally encourages head voice placement. Keep it light and bright, no pushing.
goodFor: A full octave scale on the 'Ee' vowel. The narrow tongue position and small mouth opening of 'Ee' naturally encourage head voice placement — your voice almost has to go there. Ideal for singers who get stuck in chest.

name: Vibrato Pulses
description: Hold a note and pulse the vibrato intentionally. Think of gently bouncing on the note with your diaphragm. Builds muscle memory for natural vibrato.
goodFor: Pulsing a held note with deliberate diaphragmatic bounces. You're manually creating the oscillation that vibrato does naturally — think of it as training wheels for vibrato development.

name: Sustained Vibrato Hold
description: Hold a single note and let vibrato develop naturally. Start straight, then allow oscillation to emerge. The goal is even, consistent vibrato.
goodFor: Holding a single note and allowing vibrato to emerge on its own. Unlike the pulse exercise, here you're not forcing the oscillation — you're creating the conditions (relaxed throat, steady air) for it to happen naturally.

name: Vibrato Semitone Oscillation
description: Slowly alternate between two adjacent notes to mimic the vibrato motion. Gradually speed up over time. Trains the laryngeal flexibility needed for natural vibrato.
goodFor: Slowly alternating between two notes a semitone apart, mimicking the pitch oscillation of vibrato. By starting slow and gradually speeding up, you teach your larynx the specific motion pattern that produces real vibrato.

name: Diaphragm Bounce
description: Repeated notes with deliberate diaphragmatic pulsing. Think 'huh huh huh.' Builds the breath support foundation that enables natural vibrato.
goodFor: Repeated notes with deliberate abdominal pulses ('huh huh huh'). This isolates and strengthens the specific diaphragmatic engagement that powers staccato, vibrato, and dynamic changes in singing.

name: Fast Pentatonic Run
description: Speed through the pentatonic scale up and back. The foundation of pop, R&B, and gospel riffs. Start slow, build up speed over weeks.
goodFor: Speeding through the pentatonic scale (1-2-3-5-6-8 and back). This is the exact pattern behind pop and R&B riffs — practicing it fast builds the muscle memory so riffs flow automatically when you improvise.

name: Three-Note Turn
description: A classic ornament: step up, return, step up again. This weaving pattern is the building block of every vocal run and riff. Master this first.
goodFor: A weaving three-note ornament pattern (step up, step back, step up). This tiny figure is the fundamental building block of every vocal run — once it's automatic, longer runs are just chains of this one move.

name: Descending Riff Cascade
description: Cascade down through scale tones in groups. The classic R&B and gospel descending run. Think Beyonce, Whitney, Mariah.
goodFor: A descending pattern that groups scale tones in overlapping clusters (8-7-5, 5-4-2, etc.). This is the specific rhythmic shape of classic R&B and gospel runs — the pattern that makes a run sound like a 'cascade' rather than just a fast scale.

name: Gospel Run
description: An extended fast run combining scales and a leap to the octave. The full gospel/R&B run experience. Speed comes from relaxation, not force.
goodFor: An extended run combining a scale ascent with a leap to the octave and back down. This is the full-length run pattern used in gospel and R&B — practicing it as a single phrase builds the stamina for real performance runs.

name: Straw Phonation Scale
description: Sing through a straw (or mimic the narrowing) on a 5-note scale. The gold standard of modern voice therapy. Balances airflow and vocal fold vibration effortlessly.
goodFor: Singing through a straw (or mimicking the resistance) on a 5-note scale. The narrow opening creates precise back-pressure that balances subglottic pressure and cord closure — used by voice therapists for vocal recovery and by pros for daily maintenance.

name: Sustained Vvv Buzz
description: Hold a voiced 'Vvv' fricative on a single pitch. The lip contact creates back-pressure that gently massages the vocal folds. A powerful SOVT exercise.
goodFor: Holding a voiced 'Vvv' on a single pitch. Your upper lip vibrates against your lower teeth, creating a semi-occluded tract. This specific fricative provides gentle, continuous back-pressure — like a sustained version of straw phonation.

name: Sustained Zzz Buzz Scale
description: Sing 'Zzz' through a 5-note scale. The voiced fricative activates forward resonance and creates gentle back-pressure for healthy phonation.
goodFor: Singing 'Zzz' through the 5-note scale. The tongue-to-teeth contact creates forward buzzing that you can literally feel on your lips and teeth — instant biofeedback for whether your resonance is placed forward or stuck in your throat.

name: Ma Me Mi Mo Mu Scale
description: Each ascending note gets a different vowel: Ma-Me-Mi-Mo-Mu. Trains smooth vowel transitions while maintaining forward placement through the nasal M consonant.
goodFor: Each of the five ascending notes gets a different vowel: Ma-Me-Mi-Mo-Mu. Because you change vowel on every single note, you're forced to reshape your mouth quickly while maintaining the same resonance and forward placement.

name: Bah Onset Exercise
description: Sing 'Bah' on repeated notes. The bilabial plosive B builds clean cord closure without glottal attack. One of the most universal onset exercises in vocal pedagogy.
goodFor: Repeating 'Bah' on five notes at the same pitch. The B consonant brings your lips together, naturally building cord closure before the vowel releases. This trains the clean, balanced onset that avoids both breathiness and hard glottal attacks.

name: Vee Resonance Scale
description: Sing 'Vee' through the 5-note scale. The labiodental V activates forward resonance and mask placement. Used by speech therapists and vocal coaches alike.
goodFor: Singing 'Vee' through the 5-note scale. The V consonant creates a forward buzz at your lip-teeth contact point, then the 'ee' vowel keeps the resonance narrow and high. Dual forward-placement training in one syllable.

name: Wah Open Throat Scale
description: Sing 'Wah' through the 5-note scale. The W glide naturally opens the throat while the Ah vowel encourages relaxed, open resonance. Great for releasing tension.
goodFor: Singing 'Wah' through the 5-note scale. The W starts with rounded lips and a low larynx, then the Ah opens wide — this specific lip-to-open sequence naturally stretches your throat open without you having to think about it.

name: Gentle Descending Hum
description: Softly hum from the 5th down to the root. A gentle cool-down that releases tension and brings the voice back to a relaxed state after intensive practice.
goodFor: A soft hum from the 5th down to the root at low volume. After an intense practice session, this returns your voice to a resting state — the closed mouth and descending pitch gradually reduce the energy your folds are producing.

name: Low Range Sigh
description: A gentle chromatic sigh from octave down to root. Like the yawn-sigh but specifically for cooling down. Keep it light, breathy, and completely tension-free.
goodFor: A gentle chromatic sigh spanning a full octave downward. Unlike the yawn-sigh which focuses on releasing tension, this targets the low range specifically — keeping those lower notes resonant and supported during cool-down.

name: Cool-Down Lip Trill
description: Gentle descending lip trill to wind down your voice. The bubbling releases any remaining tension in the lips and jaw. Use minimal effort.
goodFor: A descending lip trill from the 5th to the root. Combines the tension-releasing benefits of lip trills with a descending pattern that gradually reduces vocal effort — bringing your voice to rest without just stopping cold.

name: Quiet Sustained Oo
description: Hold a very quiet 'Oo' on a comfortable pitch. Pianissimo singing is the perfect cool-down — it requires minimal cord contact and gentle airflow. End your session here.
goodFor: Holding a very quiet 'Oo' at a comfortable pitch. Pianissimo singing requires the least cord contact and gentlest airflow — this is the vocal equivalent of stretching after a workout, bringing everything back to baseline.

name: Chest Voice Power Scale
description: Sing 'Hey' with strong chest voice resonance on the 5-note scale. Stay in your lower range and focus on a full, grounded sound. Build the foundation of your voice.
goodFor: Singing 'Hey' with full chest resonance on the 5-note scale, staying deliberately in your lower register. This builds the foundation of your voice's power — the rich, grounded sound that everything else sits on top of.

name: Hey Call Exercise
description: Call out 'Hey!' on the root then the fifth. Like calling across a room. Builds projection, forward placement, and chest voice power through speech-like sounds.
goodFor: Calling 'Hey!' from root to fifth, like projecting your voice across a room. This speech-like exercise activates your natural projection instincts — the ones you already use when calling someone — and applies them to singing.

name: Minor 3rd Interval Jump (1-b3-1-b3-1)
description: Jump a minor third — the soulful, bluesy interval. Think of the first two notes of 'Greensleeves.' Trains your ear for this essential interval.
goodFor: Bouncing between root and b3 (3 semitones) — the minor third that opens 'Smoke on the Water' and countless blues licks. This small but distinctive interval defines the minor sound and appears in almost every genre.

name: Major 3rd Interval Jump (1-3-1-3-1)
description: Jump a major third — the bright, happy interval. Think of the first two notes of 'When the Saints Go Marching In.' Clean and precise.
goodFor: Jumping root to major 3rd (4 semitones) and back — the bright interval that opens 'Kumbaya' and 'When the Saints Go Marching In.' Slightly wider than the minor third, it requires a precise ear adjustment between the two.

name: Minor 7th Interval Jump (1-b7-1-b7-1)
description: Jump a minor seventh — a wide, dramatic interval. Think of the opening of 'Somewhere' from West Side Story. Challenges your range and intonation.
goodFor: Leaping 10 semitones (a minor seventh) — the dramatic interval that opens 'Somewhere' from West Side Story. This wide jump challenges both your range and your ability to place a distant pitch without scooping up to it.

name: Pa-Ta-Ka Articulation
description: Alternate Pa-Ta-Ka on repeated notes. Each consonant activates different articulators: Pa (lips), Ta (tongue tip), Ka (tongue back). The gold standard of diction exercises.
goodFor: Rapidly alternating Pa-Ta-Ka on repeated notes. P uses your lips, T uses your tongue tip, K uses your tongue back — each consonant activates a completely different articulator. The fastest way to build mouth coordination for clear lyrics.

name: Tongue Twister Scale
description: Sing 'Bri-Bri-Bri' or 'Dri-Dri-Dri' through the 5-note scale. Consonant clusters force precise tongue coordination at speed. Essential for clear diction while singing.
goodFor: Singing consonant clusters like 'Bri-Bri-Bri' or 'Dri-Dri-Dri' through the 5-note scale. The double consonants force your tongue to reset quickly between each note — building the speed needed for crisp diction in fast-moving songs.

name: Rapid Consonant Switch
description: Alternate 'Bee-Dee-Gee' rapidly through the scale. Each consonant shifts the point of articulation forward to back. Builds independent control of lips, tongue tip, and tongue back.
goodFor: Alternating 'Bee-Dee-Gee' through the scale, switching between lip (B), tongue-tip (D), and tongue-back (G) consonants on every note. This trains each articulator to fire independently without the others locking up.

name: Legato Phrase Exercise
description: Sing 'La' in a long, connected phrase over an octave and back. Focus on seamless legato — no breaks, no bumps, just a smooth line of sound from start to finish.
goodFor: Singing 'La' in one long connected phrase over a full octave and back. No breaks, no breaths in the middle — just one sustained line. This trains the breath management and smooth connection that separates singing from just hitting notes.
